title: Make Good: The End-of-Lease Clause That Catches Most Tenants Off Guard
excerpt: Make-good clauses sit quietly in your lease for years — then land a five- or six-figure bill when you go to leave. Here's what they mean and how to negotiate them before you sign.
author: Joe Kelley
metaTitle: What is a Make Good Clause in a Commercial Lease? | Your Office Space
metaDescription: Make-good clauses require tenants to restore premises at lease end. Learn what they mean, what they cost, and how to negotiate better terms before you sign.
body:
Most business owners sign a commercial lease, move in, and get on with running their business. The make-good clause sits quietly in the fine print for two, three, five years — until the day you go to leave.

That's when it lands.

## What is a make-good clause?

A make-good clause requires you, as the tenant, to return the premises to the condition it was in at the start of your lease when you vacate. In a standard commercial tenancy, that means removing all your fit-out — partitions, joinery, cabling, lighting, signage — and patching, painting, and restoring everything back to base building.

Depending on how much fit-out you put in, and how long you've been there, that cost can be anywhere from $10,000 to over $200,000.

## Fair wear and tear — and why it matters

The phrase "fair wear and tear" is the key negotiating lever. A lease that requires reinstatement to original condition minus fair wear and tear is significantly better than one that requires full reinstatement regardless.

Fair wear and tear means:
- Normal scuffing on walls and flooring from everyday use is accepted
- Light fittings that have aged naturally don't need replacing
- Carpet worn from foot traffic doesn't need full replacement

What it doesn't cover:
- Structural alterations you made
- Joinery or partitions you installed
- Damage beyond normal use

## The reinstatement trap

Some leases contain full reinstatement clauses — meaning you must restore the space to exactly the condition it was in at the start of the lease, regardless of fair wear and tear. On a tenancy where you've invested $150,000 in fit-out, that clause can hand the entire cost back to you on exit.

If you've taken a base building space and improved it, you may be required to demolish everything you installed — even if the landlord would happily keep the fit-out for the next tenant.

## How to negotiate

The time to negotiate make-good is before you sign — not when you hand back the keys. Here's what to push for:

- **Cap the obligation** — agree a maximum dollar figure for reinstatement works upfront
- **Carve out your fit-out** — negotiate that existing fit-out can remain at the landlord's election
- **Cash settlement option** — agree the landlord can accept a cash payment in lieu of physical reinstatement
- **Fair wear and tear standard** — ensure the clause includes fair wear and tear exclusions explicitly
- **Photographic schedule** — document the condition of the premises at the start and get it signed off

## What a tenant representative does here

This is one of the most valuable places a tenant rep earns their fee. We read make-good clauses every week. We know what's standard in the current market, what landlords will accept, and where the leverage sits in a negotiation.

In most cases, a well-negotiated make-good clause saves more than the cost of the entire representation engagement.

If you're looking at a lease and want a second set of eyes on the make-good clause specifically — that's what we're here for.
